summary: Exceptional audience recording with crystal‑clear sound and a stellar second set featuring High Time, Jack Straw and an unforgettable Lost Sailor / Saint split.
review: The Richmond Coliseum AUD capture is widely praised as one of the best audience tapes of ’85 – crisp, full‑bodied and faithful to the live vibe, despite a few complaints about muddiness on the Oade Mac. Musically the show shines, especially the second set’s odd but inspired sequence (High Time > He’s Gone > Spoonful > Comes A Time) and the magical Lost Sailor / Saint split, earning glowing remarks for Jerry’s vocals and guitar, Phil’s bass, Bob’s lyrical touches and Mickey’s driving drums.

ai_rating.rationale: 22 reviews overwhelmingly rate the show 4‑5 stars; the majority praise both performance and recording quality, with only a single low‑rating comment about sound dullness.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Most reviewers describe the AUD as crystal‑clear, accurate to floor level, and full of ambience. A few note that the Oade Mac source sounds a bit dull with muddy bass, but overall the tape is considered one of the best audience recordings of 1985.

show_quality.setlist_flow: The second‑set list is unusual yet highly effective, delivering a string of ballads and deep jams that keep the energy high. Reviewers note the seamless transitions (e.g., Lost Sailor/Space/Saint) and the overall flow as one of the show’s strongest points.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s vocals are noted as clear and lyrical; his guitar solo on High Time is called beautiful and moving.
band_member_comments.Phil: Phil’s bass work gets high praise – “sublime”, “sick” – especially the Phil Bombs in Jack Straw and his overall interplay.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s Spoonful cover and his lyrical pull‑backs are highlighted as standout moments.
band_member_comments.Keys: Mentions of ‘Keith stuff’ show appreciation for keyboards, but no specific detail beyond general enjoyment.
band_member_comments.Drums: Mickey is noted for being forced back to the kit by set changes; pre‑drums sections are described as amazing and driving.
